findings.summary: Both rifampicin and curcumin (the principal curcuminoid of Curcuma longa) can cause hepatocellular injury; rifampicin-induced hepatotoxicity (0.6–4% of patients) and curcumin's pro-oxidant activity at high supplemental doses have been documented independently; co-administration raises concern for additive hepatotoxicity, and elevated liver enzymes have been reported in patients combining both.
findings.citation: Tostmann et al., Drug Saf 2008 (rifampicin hepatotoxicity review); Srimal, Fitoterapia 1997

findings.summary: Curcumin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein activity in hepatic and intestinal microsomes; rifampicin is partially metabolised by CYP3A4 before its own induction effect fully develops; during the first few days of co-administration, curcumin may transiently increase rifampicin plasma concentrations, elevating toxicity risk before steady-state induction is established.
findings.citation: Obach et al., Drug Metab Dispos 2006; rifampicin autoinduction kinetics

findings.summary: Curcumin is widely used by TB patients in India as a complementary remedy and is explicitly mentioned in patient surveys; clinicians should ask about turmeric/curcumin supplement use in all patients starting rifampicin-based TB regimens and advise restricting curcumin supplementation to culinary (low) doses during treatment.
findings.citation: Gupta et al., Indian J Tuberc 2010 (patient survey); RNTCP guidance
